narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared a solid profile with your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 37—which is right around the typical score for students in this dataset—upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of applicants at 92.7%. Your Year 1 school decile of 5 is a bit below the average of 5.71, while your Year 3 decile of 7 exceeds the typical 5.53, showing real improvement over time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 59.2% chance you'll fail the bar on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as schools prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your undergraduate GPA, which the model saw as slightly lower than the average 3.21 and a strong signal of potential challenges ahead. Next in impact was your Year 1 decile ranking, viewed as middling compared to peers, further tilting the odds toward failure in the model's eyes. Positively, your stronger Year 3 decile worked against that trend, pulling the prediction back toward passing and highlighting your upward trajectory. While your LSAT and family background played supporting roles, these top three academic markers dominated the decision, outweighing other strengths in your Hispanic background and full-time commitment. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—many students with similar starts have thrived by addressing early hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT for a boost or strengthening recent academics through a post-bac program to shift future predictions. You might also seek feedback from admissions counselors on bolstering your narrative around that Year 1 performance. With targeted improvements, reapplying next cycle could flip this outcome, proving the model's view isn't destiny. Your resilience, already evident in climbing deciles, positions you well for that.
